Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":88,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Los Angeles","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"no formal education","parent_2":"upper secondary"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":3,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Buddhism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["birdwatching","board games"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":80,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":91,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":72,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":82,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"In my younger days, we didn't have these fancy social media platforms. We had newspapers, radio, and television, and even then, we had to be careful about what we believed. But now, with the internet and social media, it's like we're drowning in information, and not all of it is true. I've seen my grandchildren get all worked up over something they read on Facebook, only to find out later it was just some made-up story. It's important to take a step back and think before you believe everything you see online. I like to birdwatch, and I've noticed that sometimes, the birds I see in pictures online aren't even real. They're just Photoshopped or something. It's the same with news and information. You have to be careful and check your sources. I've heard about this thing called 'fake news,' and it's a real problem. People share things without thinking, and before you know it, the whole world is believing a lie. I think we all have a responsibility to be more careful about what we share and believe online. It's like my grandmother used to say, 'If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is.' I try to live by that. I don't spend much time on social media, just an hour or so a day, but even in that time, I've seen things that make me scratch my head. I think we all need to be more like detectives, questioning and verifying before we believe and share. It's not just about us, it's about our children and grandchildren too. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: In my younger days, we didn't have these fancy social media platforms. We had newspapers, radio, and television, and even then, we had to be careful about what we believed. But now, with the internet and social media, it's like we're drowning in information, and not all of it is true. I've seen my grandchildren get all worked up over something they read on Facebook, only to find out later it was just some made-up story. It's important to take a step back and think before you believe everything you see online. I like to birdwatch, and I've noticed that sometimes, the birds I see in pictures online aren't even real. They're just Photoshopped or something. It's the same with news and information. You have to be careful and check your sources. I've heard about this thing called 'fake news,' and it's a real problem. People share things without thinking, and before you know it, the whole world is believing a lie. I think we all have a responsibility to be more careful about what we share and believe online. It's like my grandmother used to say, 'If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is.' I try to live by that. I don't spend much time on social media, just an hour or so a day, but even in that time, I've seen things that make me scratch my head. I think we all need to be more like detectives, questioning and verifying before we believe and share. It's not just about us, it's about our children and grandchildren too. We have a duty to pass on the truth, not the lies.
